description: 30-slide DALP presentation for UAE banking executives, emphasizing composability, regulatory alignment, and asset class coverage

slides.title: DALP for UAE Banks
slides.subtitle: Digital Asset Lifecycle Platform for regulated financial institutions | April 2026

slides.title: UAE Market Context
slides.subtitle: A region building digital asset infrastructure at pace

slides.title: UAE digital assets regulatory landscape
slides.body:
• The UAE has built one of the most structured regulatory environments for digital assets globally, with multiple complementary frameworks operating in parallel.
• CBUAE oversees tokenized deposits, payment tokens, and dirham-denominated instruments. SCA regulates securities tokenization across the UAE mainland.
• VARA in Dubai provides a dedicated virtual asset regulatory authority with licensing categories spanning exchanges, brokers, custodians, and issuers.
• ADGM and DIFC operate as international financial free zones with their own securities and digital asset frameworks, attracting global institutions.
• This multi-regulator environment creates specific requirements: platforms must support configurable compliance rules that adapt to each authority.

slides.title: What UAE banks need from digital asset infrastructure
slides.item1:
Regulatory flexibility
Support CBUAE, SCA, VARA, ADGM, and DIFC requirements within one operating model. Different instruments, different regulators, one platform.
slides.item2:
Sharia compliance readiness
Islamic finance instruments require specific structuring: profit-sharing, asset-backing, and prohibition of interest. The platform must accommodate these constraints.
slides.item3:
Operational governance
UAE banks operate under strict internal controls. Maker-checker approvals, RBAC, audit trails, and segregation of duties are non-negotiable.
slides.item4:
Multi-asset scalability
Banks want to start with one asset class (e.g., Sukuk or deposits) and extend to real estate, precious metals, and trade finance on the same platform.

slides.title: Key opportunities for UAE banks
slides.item1:
Sukuk tokenization
The UAE is the second-largest Sukuk market globally. Tokenized Sukuk can improve distribution, settlement speed, and investor access.
slides.item2:
Real estate tokenization
Dubai's real estate market is already exploring fractional ownership. Tokenization enables broader investor participation with governed transfer controls.
slides.item3:
Tokenized deposits
CBUAE's regulatory guidance on payment tokens and deposit tokenization opens a clear path for banks to modernize settlement infrastructure.
slides.item4:
Precious metals
Dubai Gold Souk and DMCC position the UAE as a global metals hub. Tokenized gold and silver with vault-linked metadata serve institutional and retail demand.
slides.item5:
Trade finance
Cross-border trade corridors between UAE, India, China, and Africa present opportunities for tokenized receivables and letters of credit.
slides.item6:
Fund tokenization
ADGM and DIFC-regulated fund structures can benefit from tokenized subscriptions, automated NAV distribution, and governed investor eligibility.

slides.title: UAE banks are moving from exploration to execution
slides.body: The conversation in UAE banking has shifted from "should we explore tokenization" to "which platform can support regulated operations at scale." Banks need infrastructure that fits their existing governance, connects to core banking systems, and supports multiple regulators and asset classes from day one. That is exactly the problem DALP solves.

slides.title: DALP Platform Overview
slides.subtitle: One platform for the entire digital asset lifecycle

slides.title: DALP: Digital Asset Lifecycle Platform
slides.body:
• DALP is SettleMint's platform for designing, launching, and operating tokenized assets with governance, identity, compliance, settlement coordination, and servicing on one control plane.
• It is not a tokenization engine. Tokenization is one step. DALP manages the full lifecycle: from instrument design through issuance, transfer, servicing, and redemption.
• The platform supports seven pre-built asset classes (bonds, equity, funds, deposits, stablecoins, real estate, precious metals) and allows entirely custom asset types.
• DALP is built for regulated institutions that need repeatability, auditability, and operational discipline rather than isolated proofs of concept.

slides.title: The five lifecycle pillars
slides.col1:
Design and configure
• Choose asset class or create custom types.
• Define metadata, terms, and pricing.
• Attach compliance modules and permissions.
• Review and deploy through governed workflow.
slides.col2:
Launch and distribute
• Mint tokens under controlled supply rules.
• Verify investor identity and eligibility.
• Enforce transfer conditions before execution.
• Coordinate with custody providers.
slides.col3:
Operate and service
• Run lifecycle events: payouts, freezes, redemptions.
• Monitor with full observability stack.
• Maintain audit trails for all actions.
• Extend the model to new asset classes.

slides.title: Platform architecture at a glance
slides.body: DALP provides a unified control plane that sits between your core banking systems and EVM-compatible networks. The dashboard gives operations teams real-time visibility into assets, transactions, and compliance status across all deployed instruments. APIs and event streams connect DALP to custody, identity, and settlement infrastructure.

slides.title: Composable Token Design
slides.subtitle: One contract, any instrument. The core differentiator.

slides.title: One contract type for any financial instrument
slides.body:
• DALP uses a single configurable contract type (DALPAsset) that can represent any financial instrument by combining pluggable token features and compliance modules.
• There are no fixed token types. Features and compliance rules are building blocks that can be mixed, matched, added, and removed at any time, even after deployment.
• This composability means a UAE bank can configure a Sukuk with profit-sharing features and Sharia-compliant transfer rules, then reuse the same platform for a tokenized deposit with entirely different compliance parameters.
• No redeployment needed. No separate contract per instrument. One architecture that adapts to every product.

slides.title: 11 token features across 4 categories
slides.item1:
Fees and charges
Transaction Fee, Transaction Fee Accounting, External Transaction Fee, AUM Fee. Configurable collection, reconciliation, and management fee logic.
slides.item2:
Governance and snapshots
Historical Balances, Voting Power, Permit (EIP-2612). Point-in-time queries, delegated governance, and gasless approvals.
slides.item3:
Lifecycle and yield
Maturity Redemption and Fixed Treasury Yield. Bond maturity workflows and fixed-rate yield distribution to holders.
slides.item4:
Transformation
Conversion and Conversion Minter. Support convertible instruments with automated burn-and-mint mechanics.
slides.item5:
How features work
Each feature implements lifecycle hooks: canUpdate, onTransferred, onMinted, onBurned, onRedeemed, onAttached. Up to 32 features per token.
slides.item6:
Post-deployment changes
Features can be added or removed on live tokens via setFeatures(). No redeployment. Tokens evolve as the product evolves.

slides.title: 12 compliance modules across 6 categories
slides.item1:
Geographic restrictions
Country Allow List and Country Block List. Control which jurisdictions can hold the asset.
slides.item2:
Identity access control
Identity Allow List, Identity Block List, Address Block List. Granular control over who can receive tokens.
slides.item3:
Claim-based verification
SMART Identity Verification with RPN expression engine. Evaluate KYC, AML, ACCREDITED, CONTRACT, JURISDICTION with logical operators.
slides.item4:
Supply and investor limits
Token Supply Limit (rolling-window caps), Investor Count, Capped (hard supply maximum). Match MiCA, Reg D, and local requirements.
slides.item5:
Time-based rules
Time Lock module enforces minimum holding periods using FIFO batch tracking. Configure for any jurisdiction requirement.
slides.item6:
Transfer controls
Transfer Approval (pre-authorization with configurable expiry) and Collateral (on-chain collateral ratio enforcement).

slides.title: UAE-specific compliance configuration
slides.body: DALP ships with seven regulatory compliance templates out of the box, and organizations create custom templates via the platform UI. For UAE banks, this means configuring CBUAE requirements for deposits, VARA rules for virtual assets, and ADGM securities compliance, all within the same platform. Sharia compliance constraints (asset-backing, profit-sharing structures) map to feature and module configuration rather than custom code.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/03 - Asset Designer/jpg/Asset Designer - Step 6 - Compliance Modules.jpg

slides.title: Compliance templates in practice
slides.body: The policy template library lets compliance teams define reusable rule sets that can be applied across multiple instruments. Each template combines identity verification, geographic restrictions, supply limits, and transfer controls into a single reviewable configuration. Teams can create UAE-specific templates that reflect CBUAE directives, VARA licensing conditions, or ADGM fund regulations without writing code.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/11 - Compliance and Policy Templates/jpg/Policy Templates - List.jpg

slides.title: Post-deployment flexibility
slides.body:
• Under governance role, live tokens can be reconfigured without redeployment: add or remove token features, add or remove compliance modules, change fee rates, modify country lists, adjust investor limits.
• This means a token can evolve: start as a simple bearer instrument, then add fee features, enable governance voting, or change compliance rules while live.
• For UAE banks, this is critical. Regulatory requirements change, new CBUAE guidance may require updated transfer controls, and Sharia boards may adjust structuring requirements.
• Competitors require contract redeployment or migration for these changes. DALP handles it through configuration.

slides.title: Asset Classes for UAE Banks
slides.subtitle: Seven pre-built types plus custom instruments

slides.title: Sukuk tokenization
slides.body: Sukuk are the most natural starting point for UAE banks entering digital assets. DALP supports Sukuk through the bond asset class with configurable features for profit distribution (Fixed Treasury Yield), maturity handling (Maturity Redemption), and Sharia-compliant transfer controls. Historical Balances enable point-in-time queries for distribution calculations. The platform enforces investor eligibility and geographic restrictions through composable compliance modules.

slides.title: Real estate tokenization
slides.body: Dubai's real estate market is already exploring fractional ownership through tokenization. DALP's real estate asset class supports property metadata, valuation data feeds, investor eligibility controls, and governed transfer restrictions. The platform connects property-level information to token-level operations, so each fractional interest carries the data investors and regulators require. Capped supply and investor count modules enforce distribution limits.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/10 - Real Estate/jpg/Real Estate Assets - Listing.jpg

slides.title: Tokenized deposits and precious metals
slides.body: CBUAE's regulatory guidance on deposit tokenization opens a direct path for UAE banks. DALP's deposit asset class supports controlled issuance, identity-linked holdings, and integration with existing payment infrastructure. For precious metals, DALP connects vault metadata (metal type, custodian, storage location) to token operations. Dubai's position as a global gold hub makes tokenized metals with verifiable provenance a strong commercial proposition.

slides.title: Custom asset types and extensibility
slides.col1:
Built-in asset presets
• Bond (Sukuk): yield, maturity, historical balances.
• Equity: voting power, historical balances.
• Fund: AUM fee, voting, historical balances.
• Deposit: historical balances.
• StableCoin: collateral, historical balances.
• Real Estate: capped, historical balances.
• Precious Metal: historical balances.
slides.col2:
Create any instrument type
• DALPAsset accepts any assetTypeName string.
• Configure trade finance, carbon credits, loyalty points.
• Combine any features and compliance modules.
• Define custom metadata schemas.
• Register custom features via addon registry.
• No code changes for new instrument types.

slides.title: Security architecture and access control
slides.item1:
Identity and verification
OnchainID with reusable claims. Verified identity governs who may subscribe, hold, and transfer. Distribution based on eligibility, not wallet addresses.
slides.item2:
Role-based access control
RBAC and ABAC with maker-checker workflows. Separate configuration, approval, and execution responsibilities across teams.
slides.item3:
Custody integration
Bring-your-own-custodian model supports Fireblocks, DFNS, and other providers. DALP is the lifecycle layer, not the custodian. HSM compatibility for key management.
slides.item4:
Observability and audit
Full-stack monitoring: metrics (VictoriaMetrics), logs (Loki), traces (Tempo), pre-built Grafana dashboards, and automated alerting. Every action carries an audit trail.

slides.title: Identity-linked operations
slides.body: DALP ties verified identity to operational permissions. Wallet addresses alone are never the control model. OnchainID and reusable claims let institutions control who may receive, hold, and move a regulated token. This supports cleaner onboarding, consistent transfer decisions, and a distribution model based on verified eligibility. For UAE banks, this aligns with KYC/AML requirements across CBUAE, VARA, and ADGM frameworks.

slides.title: Integration and Deployment
slides.subtitle: Connecting DALP to existing bank infrastructure

slides.title: Core banking integration and deployment
slides.col1:
Integration architecture
• REST APIs and event streams for core banking connectivity.
• Standard interfaces for custody providers.
• Identity federation with existing IAM systems.
• Settlement coordination with payment rails.
• Reporting and reconciliation outputs for books and records.
slides.col2:
Deployment options
• Self-hosted deployment for UAE data residency requirements.
• EVM-compatible network support (public and permissioned).
• High availability architecture with documented recovery procedures.
• Infrastructure as code for repeatable deployments.
• Full observability from day one.

slides.title: Why SettleMint
slides.subtitle: Platform, not project. Partner, not vendor.

slides.title: SettleMint credentials and regional presence
slides.col1:
Platform maturity
• Founded 2016, 9 years of blockchain infrastructure delivery.
• DALP represents the fourth generation of the platform.
• Active deployments across banking, capital markets, and government sectors.
slides.col2:
Regional relevance
• Existing Middle East engagements and partnerships.
• Understanding of CBUAE, SCA, VARA, ADGM, and DIFC requirements.
• Experience with Islamic finance structuring and Sharia compliance considerations.
slides.col3:
Implementation approach
• Platform delivery, not consulting projects.
• Structured onboarding with governed launch workflows.
• Reusable templates and compliance configurations accelerate time to first asset.
• Expansion to additional asset classes on the same operating model.

slides.title: Suggested engagement path
slides.body:
• Phase 1: Discovery workshop to map priority asset classes, regulatory requirements, and integration landscape. Identify the first instrument for governed launch.
• Phase 2: Platform deployment with configured asset templates, compliance modules, identity integration, and custody provider connectivity.
• Phase 3: First asset launch under full governance: maker-checker approvals, identity verification, transfer controls, and monitoring.
• Phase 4: Extend to additional asset classes using the same platform, templates, and compliance model. Each new product reuses proven infrastructure.
